title: Find number of pairs with difference = K
description: Count pairs with given absolute difference K

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all possible pairs in the array using nested loops and count those where the absolute difference equals K. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Pairs with Absolute Difference K using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of counting all pairs (i, j) where |arr[i] - arr[j]| = K using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the problem. The intuition is straightforward: to find all pairs with a specific difference, we need to examine every possible pair in the array. Since we don't know which elements might form valid pairs, we must check all combinations. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop selects the first element of the pair, and the inner loop selects the second element from all subsequent positions (to avoid counting duplicates like (i,j) and (j,i) as separate pairs). For each pair, we compute the absolute difference and compare it to K. If they match, we increment a counter. This approach is exhaustive but inefficient because it checks n*(n-1)/2 pairs, leading to O(n²) time complexity. The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables. Connection to L1: This level implements the complete solution by directly applying the nested loop structure described in L1. Common mistakes include: 1) Counting pairs twice by not restricting j > i, 2) Forgetting to use absolute value for difference, 3) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries. Real code pattern: Initialize count = 0; for i from 0 to n-2: for j from i+1 to n-1: if abs(arr[i] - arr[j]) == K: count += 1; return count.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Nested Loop Pair Generation
ladders.desc: Generate all unique pairs (i, j) from an array where i < j using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core mechanism of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to examine all possible pairs without duplication, we need a systematic way to select indices. By using an outer loop for the first index i and an inner loop for the second index j starting from i+1, we ensure each pair is considered exactly once (since j > i avoids symmetric pairs like (i,j) and (j,i)). This generates n*(n-1)/2 unique pairs, which is the total number of combinations. The algorithm: Initialize an empty list for pairs; for i from 0 to n-2: for j from i+1 to n-1: append (arr[i], arr[j]) to pairs; return pairs. Time complexity is O(n²) as we iterate through all pairs; space complexity is O(n²) if storing all pairs, but can be O(1) if processing on the fly. Connection to L0: This pair generation is directly used in L0 to check each pair for the difference condition. Common mistakes: 1) Starting j from 0 instead of i+1, leading to duplicate checks, 2) Incorrect loop bounds causing missed pairs or index errors. Real code pattern: pairs = []; for i in range(len(arr)-1): for j in range(i+1, len(arr)): pairs.append((arr[i], arr[j])); return pairs.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Absolute Difference Comparison
ladders.desc: Check if the absolute difference between two numbers equals a target value K.
ladders.explanation: This is the central logical condition of the problem. The intuition is that for a pair of numbers (a, b), their absolute difference |a - b| represents the positive distance between them on the number line. We compare this distance to K to determine if the pair satisfies the condition. The absolute value ensures the difference is non-negative, which matches the problem's requirement for absolute difference. The algorithm is simple: given two numbers a and b, compute diff = |a - b| using absolute value function, then check if diff == K. If true, the pair is valid; otherwise, it's not. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison; space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This comparison is applied to each pair generated by the nested loops in L1. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to use absolute value, leading to incorrect results for negative differences, 2) Using subtraction without considering integer overflow in extreme cases, 3) Misunderstanding that K can be zero (requiring equal numbers). Real code pattern: def is_valid_pair(a, b, K): return abs(a - b) == K.

examples.trace: Compute diff = |7 - 3| = |4| = 4. Compare diff == K: 4 == 4 is True. Return True.
Compute diff = |5 - 9| = |-4| = 4. Compare diff == K: 4 == 3 is False. Return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array Elements with Indices
ladders.desc: Access each element in an array using its index in a loop.
ladders.explanation: This building block is fundamental for processing arrays. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can access each element sequentially using an index (position) that starts from 0. By looping from index 0 to n-1 (where n is the array length), we can examine or manipulate each element one by one. This is essential for the brute force approach because we need to select elements for pair formation. The algorithm: Initialize an empty list for processed elements; for i from 0 to len(arr)-1: access arr[i] and perform an operation (e.g., print or store); return result. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing all elements; space complexity is O(1) if not storing extra data. Connection to L2: This iteration provides the elements a and b that are passed to the difference comparison in L2. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors (e.g., using i <= n instead of i < n), 2) Modifying the array while iterating without care, 3) Confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): element = arr[i]; # process element.

examples.trace: n = 4. Initialize result string. i=0: access arr[0]=2, append '2'→result='2'; i=1: access arr[1]=4, append ', 4'→result='2, 4'; i=2: access arr[2]=6, append ', 6'→result='2, 4, 6'; i=3: access arr[3]=8, append ', 8'→result='2, 4, 6, 8'. Return result.
n = 3. result string. i=0: arr[0]=5→result='5'; i=1: arr[1]=1→result='5, 1'; i=2: arr[2]=9→result='5, 1, 9'. Return result.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Absolute Value
ladders.desc: Calculate the non-negative value of a number (its distance from zero).
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental mathematical operation used in the problem. The intuition is that absolute value removes the sign of a number, giving its magnitude or distance from zero on the number line. For example, |5| = 5 and |-5| = 5. In the context of difference, |a - b| ensures we get a positive distance regardless of which number is larger. The algorithm: Given a number x, if x >= 0, return x; else, return -x (which makes it positive). Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is applied when accessing array elements in L3 to compute differences. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to handle negative numbers, 2) Using incorrect syntax (e.g., abs() in pseudocode), 3) Confusing absolute value with other operations. Real code pattern: def abs(x): return x if x >= 0 else -x.

examples.trace: x = -8. Since -8 < 0, return -x = -(-8) = 8.
x = 3. Since 3 >= 0, return x = 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to access elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic prerequisite for the problem. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element has an index (position) starting from 0. For example, in array [10, 20, 30], index 0 holds 10, index 1 holds 20, and index 2 holds 30. Accessing an element is done by specifying its index, e.g., arr[1] returns 20. The intuition is that arrays allow organized storage and quick access to data by position. This foundation is crucial because the brute force approach relies on accessing array elements to form pairs. The algorithm: Define an array with elements; to access, use array_name[index]. Time and space complexity depend on operations but accessing is O(1). Connection to L4: Arrays provide the numbers on which absolute value operations are performed in L4. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (starting from 1), 2) Accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., index 3 in a 3-element array), 3) Confusing arrays with lists in some languages. Real code pattern: arr = [3, 5, 7]; first_element = arr[0]; # gives 3.

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0: 5, index 1: 8, index 2: 2. Access arr[1]: return element at index 1, which is 8.
Array: index 0: 100, index 1: 200. Access arr[0]: return 100.

approaches.name: Approach 2: HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a hash map to store the frequency of each element. For each element, check if element + K or element - K exists in the map to count valid pairs, handling duplicates carefully. This leverages fast lookups to avoid nested loops.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Pairs with Difference K using HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution using hash map to count pairs with absolute difference K in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this approach is to leverage fast O(1) lookups of a hash map to avoid the O(n²) nested loop approach. By storing each element's frequency in a hash map, we can efficiently check for the existence of complementary values (element + K and element - K) that would form valid pairs. The key insight is that for each element x, we need to find if x+K or x-K exists in the array. However, we must handle duplicates carefully to avoid double-counting pairs. The algorithm proceeds by first building a frequency map of all elements. Then, for each unique element in the map, we check if element+K exists. If it does, we add min(freq[element], freq[element+K]) to our count for the case where K ≠ 0. When K = 0, we need to count pairs of identical elements, which is handled by the combination formula C(n,2) = n*(n-1)/2 for each element's frequency. The time complexity is O(n) for building the map and O(n) for iterating through it, giving O(n) overall. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the frequency map. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete counting logic that uses the frequency map building from L1. Common mistakes include double-counting pairs (especially when K=0), not handling negative numbers correctly (absolute difference handles this), and incorrect handling of duplicate elements. The real code pattern involves careful iteration through the map's items rather than the original array to avoid redundant checks.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize an empty dictionary freq to store element frequencies
2. For each element in arr:
   - Increment freq[element] by 1
3. Initialize count = 0
4. For each element, freq in freq.items():
   - If K == 0:
     - count += freq * (freq - 1) // 2  # combinations of identical elements
   - Else:
     - If element + K in freq:
       - count += freq * freq[element + K]
5. Return count

Note: We only check element+K (not element-K) to avoid double-counting the same pair from both directions.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build frequency map: {1:1, 5:1, 3:1, 4:1, 2:1}
Step 2: K=2 ≠ 0, iterate through map items:
- Element=1: Check if 1+2=3 exists → yes, count += 1*1 = 1 (total=1)
- Element=5: Check if 5+2=7 exists → no
- Element=3: Check if 3+2=5 exists → yes, count += 1*1 = 1 (total=2)
- Element=4: Check if 4+2=6 exists → no
- Element=2: Check if 2+2=4 exists → yes, count += 1*1 = 1 (total=3)
Step 3: Return count=3
Valid pairs: (1,3), (3,5), (2,4)

Step 1: Build frequency map: {1:3, 2:2}
Step 2: K=0, handle identical pairs:
- Element=1, freq=3: count += 3*(3-1)//2 = 3*2//2 = 3
- Element=2, freq=2: count += 2*(2-1)//2 = 2*1//2 = 1
Step 3: Total count=3+1=4
Valid pairs: Three pairs from three 1's: (1a,1b), (1a,1c), (1b,1c); One pair from two 2's: (2a,2b)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Building and Using Frequency Map
ladders.desc: Create frequency map and use it to find complementary values for pair counting.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine forms the core data structure manipulation for the HashMap approach. The intuition is that by counting how many times each element appears, we can efficiently determine how many pairs can be formed without iterating through all possible combinations. The frequency map transforms the problem from searching through arrays to looking up keys in a dictionary. The step-by-step process involves: 1) Initializing an empty dictionary, 2) Iterating through the input array once to populate the dictionary with element frequencies, 3) Using this dictionary to check for the existence of complementary values (element+K). The time complexity for building the map is O(n) for the single pass through the array. Space complexity is O(n) in the worst case when all elements are distinct. This connects to L0 by providing the essential data structure needed for the complete solution. Common mistakes include modifying the dictionary while iterating through it (which can cause errors), not handling the case when K=0 specially (where we need combinations of identical elements), and incorrectly assuming the map only needs to store presence (rather than frequency) which fails with duplicate elements. The real code pattern shows how to build the map concisely and then use it for lookups.

Algorithm:
1. freq = {}
2. For num in arr:
   - freq[num] = freq.get(num, 0) + 1
3. For num in freq:
   - If K == 0: handle specially
   - Else: check if num+K in freq

This sub-problem focuses specifically on the map operations, separate from the complete pair counting logic.

examples.input: arr = [2, 4, 6, 4, 8, 2], build map and check complements for K=2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Build frequency map:
- Process 2: freq[2]=1
- Process 4: freq[4]=1
- Process 6: freq[6]=1
- Process 4: freq[4]=2
- Process 8: freq[8]=1
- Process 2: freq[2]=2
Final map: {2:2, 4:2, 6:1, 8:1}
Step 2: Check complements for K=2:
- For element=2: Check if 2+2=4 exists → yes
- For element=4: Check if 4+2=6 exists → yes
- For element=6: Check if 6+2=8 exists → yes
- For element=8: Check if 8+2=10 exists → no

examples.input: arr = [5, 5, 5, 5], build map and handle K=0
examples.output: Map: {5:4}; For K=0: combinations from frequency 4 = 6
examples.trace:
Step 1: Build frequency map:
- Process 5: freq[5]=1
- Process 5: freq[5]=2
- Process 5: freq[5]=3
- Process 5: freq[5]=4
Final map: {5:4}
Step 2: Handle K=0 case:
- Element=5 has frequency=4
- Number of pairs from identical elements = 4*(4-1)/2 = 4*3/2 = 6

ladders.title: Core Logic — Complementary Value Lookup for Pair Formation
ladders.desc: The insight that pairs (a,b) with difference K satisfy |a-b|=K, so b = a±K.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic reveals the mathematical transformation that makes the HashMap approach possible. The intuition starts with the problem definition: we need pairs (a,b) such that |a-b| = K. This absolute value equation has two cases: either a-b = K (so b = a-K) or b-a = K (so b = a+K). However, since we're dealing with unordered pairs (a,b) is the same as (b,a), we can standardize by always looking for the larger element. The key insight is that for each element a, if we can quickly check whether a+K exists in the array, we'll find all valid pairs without duplication. We don't need to check a-K separately because that would be the same pair discovered from the perspective of the other element. This reduces the problem from checking all O(n²) pairs to making O(n) lookups. The step-by-step reasoning: 1) For each element a, calculate target = a+K, 2) Check if target exists in the data structure, 3) If it exists, count this as a valid pair. The time complexity for this lookup logic is O(1) per element when using a hash map. Space complexity is O(1) for the logic itself (not counting storage). This connects to L1 by providing the mathematical justification for why we build a frequency map and look up a+K. Common mistakes include checking both a+K and a-K (which causes double-counting), not considering that a and a+K might be the same value when K=0 (requiring special handling), and misunderstanding that we're looking for unordered pairs (so (a,b) and (b,a) are the same). The real code pattern shows the simple conditional check: if (element + K) in map.

Algorithm:
For each element a in unique elements:
  target = a + K
  if target in frequency_map:
    # Valid pair found

This logic works because if a and b form a valid pair with difference K, then either b = a+K or a = b+K. By always looking forward (a+K), we catch each pair exactly once.

examples.input: Using logic: for each element, check if element+K exists. Elements: [1, 3, 5], K=2
examples.trace:
Step 1: For element=1, target=1+2=3. 3 exists → pair (1,3)
Step 2: For element=3, target=3+2=5. 5 exists → pair (3,5)
Step 3: For element=5, target=5+2=7. 7 doesn't exist → no pair
Total pairs: (1,3) and (3,5)
Note: We don't get (5,3) because we only check forward (element+K)

examples.input: Using logic: for each element, check if element+K exists. Elements: [4, 4, 4, 6], K=2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Consider unique elements [4,6]
Step 2: For element=4, target=4+2=6. 6 exists → each 4 pairs with each 6
Step 3: For element=6, target=6+2=8. 8 doesn't exist → no pair
Total pairs: With frequencies 4:3 and 6:1, we get 3*1=3 pairs of (4,6)
Note: The three 4's each pair with the single 6

ladders.title: Building Block — Hash Map/Dictionary Operations
ladders.desc: Creating, updating, and querying hash maps for frequency tracking.
ladders.explanation:
This building block covers the fundamental data structure operations needed for the HashMap approach. The intuition is that hash maps (dictionaries) provide average O(1) time complexity for insertions, updates, and lookups, making them ideal for tracking which elements exist and how many times they appear. A hash map works by using a hash function to convert keys into array indices, allowing direct access to stored values. For frequency tracking, we use the array elements as keys and their counts as values. The step-by-step operations include: 1) Initializing an empty dictionary, 2) For each element in the input, checking if it already exists as a key in the dictionary, 3) If it exists, incrementing its value by 1; if not, adding it with value 1, 4) Later querying the dictionary to check if a specific key exists and retrieving its value. The time complexity for each insertion/update is O(1) on average. Space complexity is O(n) for storing n key-value pairs. This connects to L2 by providing the actual implementation mechanism for the complementary value lookups. Common mistakes include using lists instead of dictionaries for lookups (which would be O(n) per lookup), not handling the 'get with default' pattern correctly, and modifying dictionary size during iteration. The real code pattern in Python uses dictionary.get(key, default) to simplify frequency counting.

Algorithm:
1. freq = {}  # Initialize empty dictionary
2. For each num in array:
   - freq[num] = freq.get(num, 0) + 1
3. To check if value exists: if target in freq:
4. To get frequency: count = freq[target]

This building block is essential because without efficient dictionary operations, the O(n) algorithm wouldn't be possible.

examples.input: Perform dictionary operations: Start with {}, process [3, 7, 3, 5, 7]
Dictionary operations with get() method: Process [2, 2, 2, 2, 2]
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize freq = {}
Step 2: Process 2: freq.get(2,0)=0 → freq[2]=0+1=1
Step 3: Process 2: freq.get(2,0)=1 → freq[2]=1+1=2
Step 4: Process 2: freq.get(2,0)=2 → freq[2]=2+1=3
Step 5: Process 2: freq.get(2,0)=3 → freq[2]=3+1=4
Step 6: Process 2: freq.get(2,0)=4 → freq[2]=4+1=5
Final: {2:5}
The get(key, default) method returns the value if key exists, or default if not.

testCases.input: Dictionary {100:2, 200:1}, get value for 300 with default 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Iteration and Element Access
ladders.desc: Looping through arrays and accessing elements by index.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation covers the fundamental programming construct of iterating through arrays (lists) to process each element. The intuition is that to solve problems involving collections of data, we need systematic ways to examine each item. Array iteration allows us to perform operations on every element in sequence. In the context of the pair difference problem, we need to visit each element at least once to build our frequency map. The step-by-step process involves: 1) Initializing an index variable or using a loop construct, 2) Accessing each element using its position in the array, 3) Performing operations on that element (like updating a frequency count), 4) Continuing until all elements are processed. Time complexity for iterating through an array of size n is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration itself (not counting storage for the array). This connects to L3 by providing the mechanism to feed elements into the dictionary operations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (accessing indices outside array bounds), modifying the array while iterating through it (which can cause unexpected behavior), and using inefficient iteration patterns. The real code pattern shows simple for-loops or while-loops that traverse the array from first to last element.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize i = 0
2. While i < length(array):
   - current_element = array[i]
   - Process current_element
   - i = i + 1

Or using for-loop:
For element in array:
   Process element

This operation is foundational because almost all array algorithms begin with iteration.

examples.input: Iterate through array [10, 20, 30, 40] and print each element
examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, array[0]=10 → print 10
Step 2: i=1, array[1]=20 → print 20
Step 3: i=2, array[2]=30 → print 30
Step 4: i=3, array[3]=40 → print 40
Step 5: i=4, which is not < length(4), so loop ends

Step 1: Initialize sum=0
Step 2: First element=5 → sum=0+5=5
Step 3: Second element=3 → sum=5+3=8
Step 4: Third element=8 → sum=8+8=16
Step 5: Fourth element=1 → sum=16+1=17
Final sum=17

testCases.input: Array [100,200,300], iterate and check if any >150

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Data Types
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays as collections and integers as elements.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation covers the most basic prerequisites: what arrays are and how to work with integer values. The intuition is that arrays (also called lists in some languages) are ordered collections of elements where each element can be accessed by its position (index). In our problem, the input is an array of integers, and we need to understand both the array structure and the integer values it contains. Arrays typically start at index 0, so the first element is at position 0, the second at position 1, etc. Integers are whole numbers that can be positive, negative, or zero. The absolute difference between two integers is always non-negative. The step-by-step basics include: 1) Creating an array with specific elements, 2) Accessing an element at a particular index, 3) Understanding that arrays have a length (number of elements), 4) Performing basic integer arithmetic (addition, subtraction). Time and space analysis at this level is straightforward: accessing an array element by index is O(1), and storing an integer requires constant space. This connects to L4 by providing the data structure that we iterate through. Common mistakes include confusing array indices (thinking they start at 1), not understanding that arrays can contain duplicates, and mishandling integer overflow in some languages. The real code pattern shows simple array creation and access.

Basic concepts:
- Array: [1, 2, 3, 4] is an array of 4 integers
- Indexing: array[0] = 1, array[1] = 2, etc.
- Length: len([1,2,3]) = 3
- Integer operations: 5 + 3 = 8, |5-3| = 2

This foundation is essential because without understanding arrays and integers, one cannot even begin to understand the problem statement.

examples.input: Array [8, 3, 10, 1], access elements and compute differences
examples.trace:
Step 1: The array has 4 elements: positions 0,1,2,3
Step 2: array[0] is the first element: 8
Step 3: array[2] is the third element: 10
Step 4: Difference between 8 and 10: |8-10| = 2
Step 5: The array length is 4 (indices 0 through 3)

Step 1: Array contains 4 integer elements
Step 2: Element at index 0: 5 (positive integer)
Step 3: Element at index 1: -2 (negative integer)
Step 4: Element at index 2: 0 (zero, neither positive nor negative)
Step 5: Element at index 3: 7 (positive integer)
Step 6: All are valid integers for our problem

testCases.input: Array [4], what is the only element?

approaches.name: Approach 3: Two Pointer O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array first, then use two pointers to traverse it, counting pairs where the difference equals K by adjusting pointers based on the current difference. This avoids extra space but requires sorting.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) if sorting modifies input

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Pairs with Difference K Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete solution: sort array, then use two pointers to count pairs where absolute difference equals K.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all pairs (i, j) where i < j and |arr[i] - arr[j]| = K. The two-pointer approach after sorting provides an efficient O(n log n) solution without extra space. Intuition: Sorting brings order, allowing us to systematically explore differences between elements. After sorting, we maintain two pointers: left (starting at 0) and right (starting at 1). At each step, we compute the difference arr[right] - arr[left]. If the difference equals K, we count it and move both pointers forward (but carefully handle duplicates). If the difference is less than K, we need a larger difference, so we move right forward. If the difference is greater than K, we need a smaller difference, so we move left forward. When left == right, we increment right to avoid comparing an element with itself. The algorithm terminates when right reaches the end of the array. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting plus O(n) for the two-pointer scan = O(n log n). Space complexity is O(1) if we sort in-place, or O(n) if sorting requires extra space. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the two-pointer scanning subroutine as its core component. Common mistakes include: not handling duplicates correctly (counting duplicate pairs multiple times), off-by-one errors with pointer movement, and forgetting to sort first. To avoid duplicates, when we find a pair with difference K, we can skip over duplicate values for both pointers before counting additional pairs.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort array → [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
Step 2: Initialize left=0, right=1, count=0
Step 3: arr[right]-arr[left] = 2-1 = 1 < K=2 → move right to 2
Step 4: arr[2]-arr[0] = 3-1 = 2 = K → count=1, move left to 1, right to 3
Step 5: arr[3]-arr[1] = 4-2 = 2 = K → count=2, move left to 2, right to 4
Step 6: arr[4]-arr[2] = 5-3 = 2 = K → count=3, move left to 3, right to 5 (end)
Step 7: right=5 ≥ n=5 → stop, return count=3

Step 1: Sort → [0, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20]
Step 2: left=0, right=1, count=0
Step 3: 4-0=4=K → count=1, move left=1, right=2
Step 4: 8-4=4=K → count=2, move left=2, right=3
Step 5: 12-8=4=K → count=3, move left=3, right=4
Step 6: 16-12=4=K → count=4, move left=4, right=5
Step 7: 20-16=4=K → count=5, move left=5, right=6 (end)
Step 8: return count=5

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two-Pointer Scan on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array, use two pointers to count pairs with exact difference K.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the full solution. After the array is sorted, we need to efficiently find pairs (i, j) where arr[j] - arr[i] = K (since array is sorted, absolute difference becomes simple difference). Intuition: With sorted data, if the difference is too small, we need to increase it by moving the right pointer to a larger value. If the difference is too large, we need to decrease it by moving the left pointer to a larger value. When we find an exact match, we count it and advance both pointers. The algorithm: Initialize left=0, right=1, count=0. While right < n: If left == right, increment right (avoid self-comparison). Compute diff = arr[right] - arr[left]. If diff == K: increment count, increment left and right. If diff < K: increment right. If diff > K: increment left. Time complexity: O(n) since each pointer moves at most n times. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine, when wrapped with initial sorting, solves the full problem. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of adjusting pointers based on difference comparison. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle left == right case, which could cause infinite loop; not handling duplicates properly (may overcount). To handle duplicates: when diff == K, skip all duplicate values for both arr[left] and arr[right] before moving pointers.

examples.trace:
Step 1: left=0, right=1, count=0
Step 2: diff=2-1=1 < 2 → right=2
Step 3: diff=3-1=2 = K → count=1, left=1, right=3
Step 4: diff=4-2=2 = K → count=2, left=2, right=4
Step 5: diff=5-3=2 = K → count=3, left=3, right=5 (end)
Step 6: return count=3

Step 1: left=0, right=1, count=0
Step 2: diff=1-1=0 < 1 → right=2
Step 3: diff=2-1=1 = K → count=1, skip duplicates: left moves to first 2 (index 2), right to first 3 (index 4)
Step 4: diff=3-2=1 = K → count=2, left=3, right=5 (end)
Step 5: Also pairs (1,2) from duplicates: need careful counting → total 4 pairs: (1st1,1st2), (1st1,2nd2), (2nd1,1st2), (2nd1,2nd2)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pointer Adjustment Based on Difference Comparison
ladders.desc: Given two pointers in sorted array, decide how to move them by comparing current difference with target K.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental decision logic of the two-pointer approach. At any point, we have two indices left and right pointing to elements in a sorted array. We compute diff = arr[right] - arr[left]. We need to decide how to adjust pointers to find pairs where diff = K. Intuition: Since the array is sorted, moving right forward increases diff (because arr[right] gets larger), and moving left forward decreases diff (because arr[left] gets larger, making the subtraction smaller). So: If diff < K, we need a larger diff → move right forward. If diff > K, we need a smaller diff → move left forward. If diff == K, we found a valid pair → record it, then move both pointers forward to look for more pairs. However, we must ensure left < right always (except temporarily). This logic creates an efficient scan because each pointer moves monotonically forward, ensuring O(n) time. Connection to L1: This logic is repeatedly applied in the two-pointer scan subroutine. Connection to L3: This relies on the property that sorted order allows predictable diff changes. Common mistakes: Moving wrong pointer (e.g., moving left when diff < K would make diff even smaller); not handling the case when pointers meet. Always verify: increasing right increases diff, increasing left decreases diff.

examples.trace:
Step 1: diff = arr[1]-arr[0] = 20-10 = 10
Step 2: Compare diff=10 with K=15: diff < K
Step 3: Need larger diff → move right forward to index 2
Step 4: New diff = 30-10 = 20 > K → would then move left

Step 1: diff = arr[2]-arr[1] = 25-15 = 10
Step 2: Compare diff=10 with K=10: diff == K
Step 3: Found valid pair (15,25) → count it
Step 4: Move both pointers: left=2, right=3
Step 5: Continue scanning

ladders.title: Building Block — Working with Sorted Arrays and Differences
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array, understand how differences between elements behave and how to navigate them.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept establishes why sorting enables the two-pointer approach. In a sorted array, elements are in non-decreasing order: arr[0] ≤ arr[1] ≤ arr[2] ≤ ... For any indices i < j, the difference arr[j] - arr[i] is non-negative. Key property: If we fix i and increase j, the difference increases or stays same (if duplicate values). Conversely, if we fix j and increase i, the difference decreases or stays same. This monotonic property allows efficient searching: to find a specific difference K, we can adjust i and j systematically. Intuition: Think of two pointers as creating a 'window' between elements. Widening the window (moving j right) increases difference, narrowing it (moving i right) decreases difference. This is similar to searching in a 2D space where one axis is i and another is j. The sorted property ensures predictability. Connection to L2: This property justifies why moving right increases diff and moving left decreases diff. Connection to L4: This builds on the basic operation of computing differences. Common mistakes: Assuming strict increasing order (must handle duplicates); not recognizing that absolute difference in unsorted array becomes simple difference after sorting. Important: After sorting, |arr[i]-arr[j]| = arr[j]-arr[i] when j > i, so we only need to consider pairs with j > i.

examples.trace:
Fix i=0 (value 2):
- j=1: 5-2=3
- j=2: 7-2=5 (increased)
- j=3: 8-2=6 (increased)
- j=4: 11-2=9 (increased)
Fix j=4 (value 11):
- i=0: 11-2=9
- i=1: 11-5=6 (decreased)
- i=2: 11-7=4 (decreased)
- i=3: 11-8=3 (decreased)
Shows monotonic behavior.

With duplicates, differences can stay same:
Fix i=0 (value 1):
- j=1: 1-1=0
- j=2: 2-1=1 (increased)
- j=3: 3-1=2 (increased)
- j=4: 3-1=2 (same as previous due to duplicate 3)
Still monotonic non-decreasing when increasing j.

testCases.input: sorted_arr = [1, 3, 5, 7], i=0, move j from 1 to 3
sorted_arr = [4, 6, 8, 10], i=2, move j from 3 to 3
sorted_arr = [2, 2, 4, 4], i=1, move j from 2 to 3
sorted_arr = [10, 20, 30], fix j=2, move i from 0 to 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute and Compare Differences
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation: calculate difference between two numbers and compare it to target K.
ladders.explanation: This is the atomic operation used throughout the algorithm. Given two numbers a and b, we need to compute their difference and check if it equals a target K. Since we're working with sorted arrays and j > i, we compute diff = b - a (which is non-negative). Then we compare diff with K using standard comparison operators: if diff == K, we found a match; if diff < K, the difference is too small; if diff > K, the difference is too large. Intuition: This simple comparison drives the entire two-pointer algorithm. Every step involves computing this diff and making a decision. In the context of the problem, we're essentially searching for pairs where this computed difference equals exactly K. Connection to L3: This operation is applied repeatedly as we traverse the sorted array with two pointers. Connection to L5: This builds on basic arithmetic and comparison skills. Common mistakes: Using absolute difference unnecessarily after sorting (since sorted ensures b ≥ a); integer overflow if numbers are very large (but typically not an issue in problem constraints); floating-point errors if using non-integers (but problem usually uses integers). Always use integer arithmetic for exact comparisons.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute diff = b - a = 9 - 5 = 4
Step 2: Compare diff with K: 4 == 4
Step 3: Result: diff equals K → found match

Step 1: Compute diff = 18 - 12 = 6
Step 2: Compare diff with K: 6 < 7
Step 3: Result: diff less than K → need larger difference

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Differences and Comparisons
ladders.desc: Beginner-level: what is difference between two numbers, and how to compare numbers.
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute prerequisite for the entire problem. Difference between two numbers a and b is defined as |a - b| (absolute difference), which is always non-negative. For example, difference between 7 and 3 is |7-3| = 4, same as |3-7| = 4. In simpler terms, it's the distance between numbers on the number line. Comparison involves checking relationships between numbers: equal to (==), less than (<), greater than (>). For example, 5 == 5 is true, 5 < 8 is true, 10 > 7 is true. Intuition: Finding pairs with difference K means we're looking for two numbers that are exactly K units apart on the number line. If K=2, numbers like (1,3) or (5,7) work because 3-1=2 and 7-5=2. Connection to L4: This foundational concept is used when we compute diff = b - a and compare it to K. Common beginner mistakes: Confusing difference with other operations like sum; forgetting absolute value when needed; misunderstanding comparison operators. Practice: Given two numbers, compute their difference, then check if it equals, is less than, or is greater than a target value.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute difference: |9-4| = 5
Step 2: Compare with K=5: 5 == 5
Step 3: So difference equals K

Step 1: Compute difference: |10-6| = 4
Step 2: Compare with K=3: 4 > 3
Step 3: So difference greater than K
